does: SpeechJudge introduces a comprehensive framework for evaluating speech naturalness through three interconnected components: a large-scale human preference dataset of 99K annotated speech pairs generated by diverse zero-shot text-to-speech models across multiple languages and styles, a challenging benchmark called SpeechJudge-Eval for assessing naturalness judgment capabilities, and a reward model trained to predict human preferences for speech naturalness. The paper demonstrates that current metrics and audio language models perform poorly on this task, with the best-performing model achieving less than 70% agreement with human judgments, revealing a significant gap between machine and human evaluation of naturalness in synthesized speech.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature of aligning large generative models with human feedback, extending techniques from preference learning and reward modeling from vision and language domains into the speech synthesis domain. It addresses a critical limitation in prior TTS research—the absence of large-scale human preference data for naturalness evaluation—which has constrained the development of naturalness-aware synthesis models. The paper responds to recent advances in zero-shot text-to-speech systems and the broader challenge of establishing reliable evaluation metrics for subjective speech quality, connecting to prior work on human-centered evaluation frameworks for generative models.
enables: SpeechJudge enables future development of speech synthesis models that can be fine-tuned or trained with reinforcement learning from human feedback, leveraging the reward model as a scalable proxy for human preference. The benchmark provides a foundation for systematically improving automated speech evaluation, potentially through better audio language models or specialized speech judges. The framework supports research into cross-lingual and cross-style naturalness perception, opening directions for multilingual TTS optimization and more nuanced understanding of what constitutes natural speech across diverse acoustic conditions. The dataset and benchmark also facilitate development of improved metrics for speech quality assessment beyond naturalness alone.
